commonIssues: Stem and root rot in poorly-drained or waterlogged soil; let the top inch dry between waterings and use free-draining mix.
Bolting (premature flowering) if moisture is uneven or the plant is heat-stressed; keep soil consistently moist during bulb development.
Aphids on fresh spring foliage; usually minor.
Slugs and snails damage young seedlings.
Swallowtail butterfly caterpillars may feed on the foliage.
Has a taproot and resents transplanting/root disturbance; sow direct or transplant young to avoid transplant shock.
Container minimum (about 20 L, ~30 cm wide and deep per RHS) suits Florence/bulb and herb fennel; the plant grows tall (1.2-1.8 m), so use a heavy, stable, sheltered pot to prevent it blowing over.
Self-seeds aggressively; deadhead spent flowers to control spread.

source.citation: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Foeniculum vulgare: Zone 4 to 9, Sun = Full sun, Water = Medium, height 4-6 ft, problems include stem/root rot, aphids, slugs.
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Foeniculum vulgare: USDA hardiness Zone 4 (approx. -34 C cold limit) to 9; full sun; medium water needs.
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Foeniculum vulgare: common problems are stem and root rot (especially in poorly-drained conditions), aphids, slugs, and swallowtail butterfly caterpillars.

RHS - How to grow Fennel: grow in a large container at least 30 cm (1 ft) wide and deep filled with peat-free multi-purpose compost; use a heavy, stable, sheltered pot as fennel grows tall and can blow over.

RHS - How to grow Fennel: needs no feeding and is drought-tolerant once established; container plants need a steady supply of moisture through the growing season; full sun, warm, sheltered, free-draining soil.

RHS - How to grow Fennel: free-draining soil essential; avoid waterlogged conditions (root rot risk); container plants need steady moisture but herb fennel is hardy and drought-tolerant once established.

The Old Farmer's Almanac - Fennel: likes an inch of water per week or more but is drought tolerant once established (taproot); full sun; fertile soil high in organic matter; neutral pH 6.5-7.5; thin to 6-9 in spacing; constantly damp foliage invites fungal issues.
The Old Farmer's Almanac - Fennel: forms a taproot and can suffer extreme transplant shock if the taproot is injured; sow direct in midsummer; full-sun herb-garden/flower-bed crop.

University of Illinois Extension - Fennel: herb fennel sown in spring after frost is past; Florence fennel sown mid-June to July for cooler-season bulb development; prefers full sun and uniformly moist soil.

Confidence note: targetSunHours (7) and hotWeatherMultiplier (1.5) are conservative estimates derived from qualitative 'full sun' guidance (Missouri Bot. Garden, RHS, Almanac) standardized to the common 6-8 h/day full-sun convention; no source gave an exact hours/day figure. Feeding interval (30 days) is a conservative light-feeder estimate since RHS states fennel 'needs no feeding' while the Almanac calls it a heavy feeder in poor soil - intervals span widely by context. baseIntervalDays (4) is derived from the Almanac '1 inch/week' guideline interpreted for container drying.
